text: This video shows a laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with inferior mesenteric artery for diverticular disease. A forty-two-year-old man presented with a medical history of diverticular disease and a recent episode of complicated diverticulitis treated medically. Eight weeks after recovery, an endoscopic evaluation excluded any malignancy. An elective laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy was recommended to the patient. An abdominal CT scan with angiographic study was performed preoperatively. CT angiography showed two sigmoid branches originating from IMA. Left colic artery was not identified, whereas an accessory middle colic artery was found to originate from superior mesenteric artery and to connect with the marginal artery. This provides, together with the left branch of middle colic artery, the blood supply to the splenic flexure. An accessory middle colic artery is present in thirty-six percent of cases. Its frequency rises to eighty-five percent when the left colic artery is missing. According to some orders, accessory middle colic artery would compensate left colic artery absence in order to supply splenic flexure.

This video shows a laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with inferior mesenteric artery for diverticular disease. A forty-two-year-old man presented with a medical history of diverticular disease and a recent episode of complicated diverticulitis treated medically. Eight weeks after recovery, an endoscopic evaluation excluded any malignancy. An elective laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy was recommended to the patient. An abdominal CT scan with angiographic study was performed preoperatively. CT angiography showed two sigmoid branches originating from IMA. Left colic artery was not identified, whereas an accessory middle colic artery was found to originate from superior mesenteric artery and to connect with the marginal artery. This provides, together with the left branch of middle colic artery, the blood supply to the splenic flexure. An accessory middle colic artery is present in thirty-six percent of cases. Its frequency rises to eighty-five percent when the left colic artery is missing. According to some orders, accessory middle colic artery would compensate left colic artery absence in order to supply splenic flexure.

The surgeon applies a clip on the proximal section point identified through a suprapubic mini laparotomy. The coloepiploic detachment is performed towards the left colic angle, revealing the posterior wall of the stomach and confirming the opening of the lesser sac. The release of the trisangle allows for the identification of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), which is isolated and sectioned between clips, with its distal portion suspended upwards. The mesocolon is incised above the pancreas to enter the lesser sac, highlighting the root of the transverse mesocolon, which is sectioned along the pancreatic border. The colon is laterally mobilized upwards, completing the dissection plane for the splenic flexure mobilization. The aortic plane and the presumed origin of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) are identified.

The surgeon applies a clip on the proximal section point identified from the suprapubic mini laparotomy. The coloepiploid detachment is directed towards the left colic angle, revealing the posterior wall of the stomach and confirming the opening of the lesser sac. The release of the trisangle allows identification of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), which is isolated and sectioned between clips, with its distal portion suspended upwards. Blunt dissection maneuvers are performed in the embryological plane below the vein, extending in a medium lateral direction in the subvascular plane up to the colic wall and the lower edge of the pancreas. The mesocolon is incised above the pancreas to enter the lesser sac, highlighting and sectioning the root of the transverse mesocolon along the pancreatic border. The colon is mobilized laterally upwards, completing the dissection plane for full mobilization of the splenic flexure. The aortic plane and presumed origin of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) are identified.

The surgeon identifies the sigmoid vascular branches and incises the mesocolon laterally to the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The separation between the Toldt fascia and the Denonvilliers fascia is resumed, completing the lateral mobilization of the colon upwards to the previous dissection plane. The colon is then mobilized downwards, freeing the sigmoid colon and incising the peritoneal reflection. The first sigmoid artery is identified, coagulated, and sectioned.

The surgeon identifies sigmoid vascular branches and incises the mesocolon laterally to the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The separation between the Told and Nigerota fascia is resumed, and lateral mobilization of the colon is completed upwards, finding the previous dissection plane, then downwards, freeing the sigmoid colon and incising the peritoneal reflection. The first sigmoid artery is identified, coagulated, and sectioned. Intramesocolic dissection begins below the colonic wall and continues in contact with the bowel above the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The peritoneum is incised, marking the section line upwards and downwards to the upper third of the rectum. Sigmoid arterial branches are coagulated and sectioned individually, preserving the autonomic nerves around the IMA and those ascending from the pelvic plexus.

The surgeon performs intramesocolic dissection below the colonic wall, maintaining contact with the bowel above the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The peritoneum is incised along the section line extending upwards and downwards to the upper third of the rectum. Sigmoid arterial branches are individually coagulated and sectioned, preserving the nerves around the IMA and those ascending from the pelvic plexus.

The surgeon reaches the upper rectum, surrounding the rectum-sigma junction for resection. The section plane is maintained above the upper rectal artery, preserving the mesorectum. The colon is prepared for distal section below the rectum-sigmoid junction, and the bowel is transected using a mechanical stapler. The bowel is externalized through a suprapubic incision, and a straight mechanical colorectal anastomosis is performed. The mesocolic bridge is closed with glue, and the stapler is fired. The anastomosis is tested with transanal irrigation using methionine blue or the Ehrlich test, followed by perioperative rectoscopy to check for anastomotic bleeding.

The surgeon reaches the upper rectum, surrounding it from above and including the rectum-sigma junction in the resection. The section plane is maintained above the upper rectal artery, preserving the mesorectum. The colon is prepared for distal section below the rectum-sigmoid junction, and a mechanical stapler is used to cut the bowel. The bowel is then externalized through a suprapubic incision, and the previously placed clip identifies the proximal section margin. A straight mechanical colorectal anastomosis is performed, ensuring correct colon axis orientation. The mesocolic bridge is closed with glue, and the stapler is fired. The anastomosis is tested with transanal irrigation using methionine blue or the Ehrlich test. A perioperative rectoscopy is conducted to exclude any anastomotic bleeding.
